name: Arnett Cobb
abstract: Arnett Cleophus Cobb (August 10, 1918 – March 24, 1989) was an American tenor saxophonist, sometimes known as the "Wild Man of the Tenor Sax" because of his uninhibited stomping style. Cobb wrote the words and music for the jazz standard "Smooth Sailing" (1951), which Ella Fitzgerald recorded for Decca and later included on her album Lul…

works.t: Lady, be Good!: Oh, Lady, Be Good!

quotes.text: Yeah. I tell you what -- I played a Pittsburgh Courier thing at Carnegie Hall with him at midnight and we received awards, and I was in the running of the saxophone players for the Pittsburgh Courier. And, of course, we had to stay up until 8:00 o'clock that morning going into Columbus, and this is when I made up my mind I was going to put my notice in.
quotes.caption: 1947 Pittsburgh Courier Poll Winners Jazz Concert

quotes.text: Yes, going back with Lionel, (inaudible). We're leaving on the 29th of April. We (rehearse) (inaudible) on the 30th of June -- I mean -- 29th of April -- 29th of June and rehearse on the 30th of June and the first of July. We play a concert at Carnegie Hall on the first, that night, of July. We go to Saratoga on the 2nd, and we're off on the third and leave on the 4th, get into Nice on the 5th. We have --
quotes.caption: Arnett Cobb recalls playing with Lionel Hampton at Carnegie Hall on July 1, 1978

quotes.text: Told you about the Carnegie Hall situation, the Ralph Cooper situation and blah, blah, blah and about my mother. You know, after my wife died, I brought my mother out there with me, and she took care of me. But she was a woman of great understanding. And anytime I'd get depressed or have any problem, I could go to her and talk to her, and she'd always have something good to say. She was clean in thought and in heart, and never give you the wrong advice.
quotes.caption: Arnett Cobb recalls his mother caring for him at Carnegie Hall

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Golson
about_quotes.evidence: Incidentally, years later – many years later – it must have been 50 years later – I happened to see him in Nice, France. I said, “You’re the reason that I play the saxophone.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Benny Golson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Ed Soph
about_quotes.evidence: I was known as “Little Eddie” because all the guys that I played with when I was coming up — Arnett was —
about_quotes.doc_title: Ed Soph interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, February 9, 2024

about_quotes.speaker: Big Nick Nicholas
about_quotes.evidence: And of course when we got our act together we played. And, incidentally, Linton Garner came in, Erroll Garner's brother. But this particular thing...we had two girls and Austin Powell... Yeah. And Jumpin' George, he used to be with Arnett Cobb. He was a show drummer...

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Segal
about_quotes.evidence: I hadn’t known that he started off on alto, and when he got to Hamp’s band he said, “Where’s your tenor?” He had to go against Arnett Cobb, he said, “oh my goodness. Give me a break.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Segal — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Buddy Tate
about_quotes.evidence: Buddy Tate is one of the oldest surviving alumni of the Count Basie Orchestra... known as one of the great “Texas Tenors” with Illinois Jacquet and Arnett Cobb.
about_quotes.doc_title: Buddy Tate interviewed by Michael Woods, Scottsdale, Arizona, March 3, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Jackie McLean
about_quotes.evidence: Of course the other musicians that lived in the neighborhood is like an encyclopedia of jazz: Duke Ellington, Don Redman, Arnett Cobb.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jackie McLean — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
